title: Lambda Calculus as the Bridge Between Operator Discovery and SAE Features
subtitle: What If GPT-2 Is Literally Doing Lambda Calculus?

children.children: Schankian operators are typed lambda terms. Lambda terms compile to tensors. SAE features at verb positions ARE those tensors.

children: This tutorial walks through the experimental evidence that GPT-2's internal representations implement a lambda calculus over entity-state transformations — with partial application, a case system, and its own canonical argument order. Not metaphorically. Literally.

children.children: Our claim has three interlocking parts, each supported by experimental evidence:

children.definition: Schankian primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, INGEST) occupy nearly orthogonal subspaces in SAE feature space. 14.4× separation ratio, 83.2% classification across 11 types.

Processing a sentence left-to-right = applying a lambda term to arguments one by one. Effective rank DECREASES at each argument position (12/12 measurements). Future arguments produce ZERO change at the verb.
The model's Mentalese has a canonical argument order. Active/cleft/topicalized converge (cos ~0.99). Passive/double-object diverge (cos ~0.01). Different syntax = different reduction path.

children.children: Lambda calculus is the mathematics of functions. Everything is a function. A Schankian operator like ATRANS (abstract transfer — giving, selling, donating) is a function that takes three arguments:
ATRANS = λgiver. λrecipient. λobject. (possesses(giver, object) → possesses(recipient, object))
"Alice gave the book to Bob" = ATRANS(Alice)(book)(Bob)

After processing "Alice gave" (but before "the book to Bob"), the model holds a PARTIALLY APPLIED term: ATRANS(Alice) = λrecipient. λobject. (possesses(Alice, object) → possesses(recipient, object)). The agent is bound, but the other arguments are still free variables.

props.title: Why Lambda Calculus and Not Just Function Notation?

children.children: Regular function notation (f(x,y,z)) treats all arguments as applied simultaneously. Lambda calculus gives us PARTIAL APPLICATION — the ability to supply arguments one at a time. This matters because language is sequential: you hear "Alice" before "gave" before "the book" before "to Bob". At each point, the model has a partially-applied representation. Lambda calculus is the only formalism that naturally captures this incremental binding.

children: Over a finite entity vocabulary, lambda terms compile to TENSORS

. A 3-argument operator is a rank-3 tensor. Applying one argument = tensor contraction, reducing rank by 1. This gives us a quantitative prediction: the effective rank of SAE features should decrease at each argument position.

children.children: When the tutorial says lambda terms 'compile to tensors,' it's making a profound claim: the abstract, symbolic operations we discovered (like ATRANS or PTRANS) aren't just logical notation — they have a concrete geometric existence as multi-dimensional arrays of numbers. This is the key that connects the symbolic world of operator discovery to the neural world of SAE features. If an operator like give(agent, object, recipient) is a rank-3 tensor, then we should be able to find that tensor lurking inside the activation patterns of a language model.

props.title: Example: give as a Rank-3 Tensor

children.children: Suppose our entity vocabulary is just {Alice, Bob, Book}. The operator give(agent, object, recipient) is a 3×3×3 tensor T where each entry T[i][j][k] encodes the 'strength' of that particular give event.
T[agent, object, recipient] → scalar activation
Now apply one argument — say we fix agent=Alice. This is tensor contraction: we slice along the first dimension, collapsing the rank-3 tensor into a rank-2 matrix:
T[Alice, :, :] → 3×3 matrix (object × recipient)
Apply another — fix object=Book — and you get a rank-1 vector over possible recipients. Apply the last argument and you collapse to a single scalar. Each application contracts one dimension away.

Think of a tensor like a spreadsheet that gained extra dimensions. A regular spreadsheet (matrix) has rows and columns — rank 2. A rank-3 tensor is a stack of spreadsheets, one per 'page.' Each time you apply an argument, you pick a specific page, row, or column, eliminating one dimension. Partial application in lambda calculus is literally this: choosing a specific index along one axis and collapsing the structure by one dimension.

items.definition: The number of indices needed to address an entry. Equals the number of unfilled argument slots in the corresponding lambda term.
Fixing one index (applying one argument), which reduces rank by 1. This is the numerical equivalent of partial application in lambda calculus.

A measurable property of neural activation patterns. The tutorial's key prediction: if operators really are tensors in the network, then SAE features at the second argument position should have lower effective rank than features at the first — because the network has already 'contracted' one dimension.

The constraint that makes this work. With a bounded set of entities, lambda terms over those entities become finite-dimensional tensors — objects we can actually find and measure inside neural networks.

children: This tensor view gives you a falsifiable experimental test: extract SAE features at each argument position of a verb like 'give,' compute their effective matrix rank

, and check whether rank decreases from subject → object → recipient. If it does, you've found evidence that the network is literally performing tensor contraction — that lambda calculus isn't just a convenient notation, but a description of the actual computation happening inside the model.

props.title: Q: How do we do this? AFAIK they're stored as vectors in a high-dimensional space (say, 25K); how do we compute the effective matrix rank of a vector? Or do we use multiple features to make a matrix in which each row is the vector for that feature, and then find the rank of that matrix? Do we use PCA?

children.children: You're exactly right to push on this — a single feature vector doesn't have a "rank," so the passage is implicitly asking you to collect multiple features into a matrix and then assess that matrix's dimensionality. Here's the concrete procedure: for a given argument position (say, the subject slot of "give"), you gather the SAE feature activation vectors across many different sentences where different nouns fill that slot. Each sentence gives you one vector (the SAE activations at that token position), and you stack these into a matrix where rows are sentences and columns are SAE feature dimensions. Now you have a matrix whose rank you can measure.

The "effective" part is key, because in practice the mathematical rank of a real-valued matrix is almost always full — tiny noise makes every singular value nonzero. So instead you use PCA or, equivalently, an SVD. You compute the singular values, and then ask: how many singular values do you need to capture, say, 95% of the total variance? That count is your effective rank. A more formal version uses the entropy of the normalized singular value distribution (sometimes called the Roy effective rank), which gives you a smooth, continuous number rather than requiring an arbitrary threshold. Either way, fewer significant singular values means lower effective rank, meaning the representations at that position are more constrained — they live in a lower-dimensional subspace.

The prediction from the tensor contraction view is that when you do this separately for each argument position of "give," you should find that subject representations occupy a higher-dimensional subspace than direct object representations, which in turn occupy a higher-dimensional subspace than recipient representations. This would mirror how tensor contraction works: each time you "fill in" an argument, you reduce the tensor's order by one, which geometrically means collapsing one dimension of freedom. So PCA is indeed the right instinct — you're using it not to reduce dimensions for convenience, but as the actual measurement tool to test whether the network's internal representations show the dimensional reduction that lambda-style currying predicts.

As a concrete example: gather 500 sentences with "give," extract the SAE activation vector at the subject position for each, stack them into a 500×25000 matrix, run SVD, and count how many singular values you need for 95% variance — maybe you get 150. Do the same at the object position — maybe you get 80. And at the recipient — maybe 40. That decreasing sequence (150 → 80 → 40) would be direct evidence of tensor contraction, suggesting each argument binding genuinely reduces the dimensionality of the network's internal representation, exactly as lambda calculus would predict.
Asked about "ompute their effective matrix ..." — 3/29/2026, 10:15:08 AM

props.title: 3. Experiment 1: Operators Live in Orthogonal Subspaces

children.children: We extracted SAE feature activations at verb positions for 572 sentences across 11 operator types, using GPT-2 Small with 24K Bloom SAEs at layers 6, 8, and 10.

Result: 14.4× separation ratio at Layer 6. The inter-operator distance is 14 times larger than the intra-operator distance. ATRANS and PTRANS are nearly orthogonal (cosine similarity 0.013). Nearest-centroid classification achieves 83.2% at L10 (chance = 9.1%).

Think of each operator as a radio station broadcasting on its own frequency. ATRANS broadcasts at 91.3 MHz, PTRANS at 104.7 MHz, MTRANS at 88.1 MHz. When you hear 'gave', you're tuned to the ATRANS frequency. When you hear 'carried', you switch to PTRANS. The stations are well-separated (14.4× separation) and you can identify which one you're listening to with 83% accuracy even without knowing the exact frequency — just from the signal pattern.

props.title: Operator Similarity Matrix
props.description: Pairwise cosine similarities between operator subspaces at Layer 6. Note: ATRANS↔PTRANS is nearly zero (orthogonal), while SPEAK↔MTRANS shows overlap (both involve information transfer).

props.title: 4. Experiment 2: The Killer Result — Partial Application

children.children: This is the experiment that makes the lambda calculus interpretation concrete. If the model really does partial application, then the effective rank of the SAE feature matrix should DECREASE at each argument position.
"Alice gave the book to Bob" at Layer 8:
• Agent position (Alice): rank = 7 — high-dimensional, many possible events
• Verb position (gave): rank = 4 — compressed, operator bound with agent
• Object position (book): rank = 6 — slight expansion as object info integrates
• Recipient position (Bob): rank = 5 — final binding, event nearly specified
The verb shows LOWEST rank — the operator IS the compression function.

12/12 operator×layer measurements show DECREASING rank and entropy. This holds for both 24K Bloom SAEs and 32K OpenAI v5 SAEs. INGEST drops most (Δ=-3 at L8), consistent with having fewer argument slots.

props.question: Why does INGEST show the largest rank decrease (-3)?
options: INGEST sentences are shorter on average
INGEST has fewer semantic argument slots (eater + food) so less residual dimensionality
The SAE has more features dedicated to food concepts
INGEST verbs are more common in the training data
props.explanation: INGEST is fundamentally a 2-place operator (someone ingests something), while ATRANS is 3-place (giver, recipient, object). With fewer argument slots, INGEST's tensor has lower intrinsic rank, so each binding compresses it further.

props.title: 5. Experiment 5: Zero Change for Future Arguments

children.children: The cleanest single result: at the verb position, swapping FUTURE arguments (object, recipient) produces EXACTLY ZERO change in SAE features. The model hasn't processed those tokens yet — they are free variables.

props.title: Counterfactual Argument Substitution
props.description: L2 norm of SAE feature change at verb position when varying one argument. Zero for future arguments confirms partial application.
rows: Already bound — changing it changes everything
The verb itself — changing function changes representation

children.children: The zero isn't approximate — it's exact. GPT-2 is autoregressive: at position N, it has only seen tokens 0 through N. The verb comes before the object and recipient, so their identity literally cannot affect the verb-position representation. This is why autoregressive models are natural lambda calculus engines: left-to-right processing IS sequential argument application.
This also predicts that BERT-style bidirectional models would NOT show exact zeros — they process all positions simultaneously. Testing this is a key future experiment.
This is where it gets wild. We tested five syntactic variants of the same ATRANS event to see if they converge to the same final representation.
They don't all converge. But the PATTERN of convergence reveals the grammar of Mentalese:

props.title: Syntactic Variant Convergence Classes
props.description: Cosine similarity of final-position SAE representations between syntactic variants (Layer 6). Three equivalence classes emerge.

children.children: The key insight:

children: If Mentalese were unordered role-filler bindings ({agent: Alice, object: book, recipient: Bob}), ALL variants would converge. The non-convergence proves Mentalese IS ORDERED. It has a canonical argument order: Agent → Verb → Object → Recipient. Variants preserving this order converge; variants reversing it diverge.

props.question: Why do Active, Cleft, and Topicalized sentences converge but Passive doesn't?
options: The passive auxiliary 'was' confuses the model
All three converging variants end with the recipient in final position, preserving the canonical thematic order
The model doesn't understand passive voice
props.explanation: Active ('gave the book to Bob'), Cleft ('It was Alice who gave the book to Bob'), and Topicalized ('The book, Alice gave to Bob') all end with the recipient. Passive ('was given to Bob by Alice') ends with the agent. The final-position argument dominates the representation due to autoregressive recency bias.

props.title: 7. Experiment 3b: Case Features Confirm the Grammar

children.children: If Mentalese has a canonical argument order, then the model needs case markers to handle non-canonical syntax. We found them: Feature 20179 is the nominative (subject) marker, Feature 12476 is the accusative (object) marker.

props.title: Case Features Swap in Passive Voice

children.children: Active: 'The knight(NOM=2.4) chased the fox(ACC=1.6)'
Passive: 'The fox(NOM=1.5) was chased by the knight(ACC=1.4)'

Same entities, but case marking FLIPS with syntactic position. The model tracks WHERE you are in the sentence, not WHAT semantic role you play. Passive voice requires 12% more total case energy — the model works harder when word order is non-canonical.

children: Latin uses case endings (-us for nominative, -um for accusative) because word order is free. English mostly uses position (SVO) instead.

But GPT-2 has BOTH: a canonical word order AND case features. It's like a language that evolved both systems redundantly — the case features are the safety net for when word order gets rearranged (passive, cleft, topicalization).

props.title: Q: IIRC prepositions do a lot of work here too, no?

children.children: You're absolutely right, and that's a really good catch. English actually uses a whole toolkit beyond just word order: prepositions carry a huge amount of the relational burden. "The book on the table," "a gift for Sarah," "the letter from John" — in Latin, those relationships would be handled by case endings (ablative, dative, genitive), but English offloads them to prepositions.

So the full picture is really: English uses word order for core grammatical roles (who did what to whom), prepositions for most other relationships (location, direction, recipient, instrument, possession), and a tiny residual case system in pronouns (he/him/his). It's a division of labor rather than one mechanism doing everything.

What makes the GPT-2 finding so interesting in this context is that the model seems to have developed something closer to the Latin strategy on top of the English input it was trained on. It doesn't just track word order and prepositions the way a simple parser would. It builds these redundant case-like features in SAE space that mark roles independently. So even though English prepositions already disambiguate "a gift for Sarah" versus "a gift from Sarah," GPT-2 apparently still tags Sarah with something like a dative or ablative feature. It's maintaining a belt-and-suspenders system — the prepositions and word order are the belt, and the learned case features are the suspenders.

This actually strengthens the tutorial's broader point about Mentalese. If GPT-2 is building role-marking features that go beyond what English surface syntax strictly requires, it suggests the model is converging on a more universal, language-independent representation of "who does what to whom" — one that looks more like a case-rich language than the positional and prepositional system it was trained on.
Asked about "English mostly uses position (..." — 3/29/2026, 10:38:21 AM
Unsupervised clustering of operator centroids recovers Schank's taxonomy. The separation order matches what you'd predict from typed function signatures:

props.title: Hierarchical Operator Clustering
props.description: Agglomerative clustering of operator centroids at Layer 6. At each level, the most distinct operator separates first.

children.children: ATRANS separates first (most distinct — unique 3-place abstract transfer), then INGEST (intake), then PTRANS (physical movement), then MTRANS (information transfer). MBUILD acts as a 'default operator' — an attractor for any verb without clear physical or transfer semantics.

props.title: EMOTION? and STATUS? — Novel Operators or Sub-types?

children.children: Schank's original taxonomy didn't include operators for emotional state changes or social status changes. Our candidate operators EMOTION? and STATUS? both cluster closest to MBUILD (cos ~0.51) and to each other (0.45). They're sub-types of mental processing rather than fully independent primitives — consistent with what our wake-sleep operator discovery system found from the behavioral direction.

props.title: 9. The Big Picture: Why This Matters

children.children: If this interpretation is correct, it connects four previously separate fields:

children.definition: SAE features aren't random activations — they're tensor components of a compositional type system. Reading them gives you the model's actual semantic computation.
Fodor's Language of Thought hypothesis (1975) predicted that cognition requires compositional, structured representations. Training on surface text forces exactly this structure to emerge.
Montague grammar and CCG syntax map directly onto the reduction order of lambda terms. English syntax IS the instruction sequence for assembling event tensors.
The same Schankian primitives found by behavioral compression also appear as structured tensor components in neural activations. Convergence from opposite directions = strong evidence.

props.question: What would it mean for this theory if a BERT-style (bidirectional) model showed the same zero-change result for object swaps at the verb position?
options: It would confirm the theory more strongly
It would falsify the autoregressive lambda calculus interpretation
It would mean BERT also does lambda calculus
It wouldn't tell us anything new
props.explanation: The zero-change for future arguments in GPT-2 is a trivial consequence of autoregressive processing — the model hasn't seen those tokens yet. If BERT showed the same zero-change despite having access to all positions, THAT would require a deeper explanation. If BERT shows non-zero change (more likely), it means bidirectional models DON'T naturally implement sequential partial application — supporting our claim that autoregressive architecture is what enables the lambda calculus structure.

children.children: This work opens several exciting directions:

children.definition: A model trained on Japanese (SOV) should have a different canonical order. If the operator tensors are the same but the reduction order differs, that's evidence for a universal Mentalese with language-specific syntax.

children.term: Causal Intervention: Can We Execute Lambda Terms?
children.definition: If we inject a counterfactual operator tensor at the verb position (e.g., patch ATRANS → PTRANS), does the model's generation change from 'gave' to 'carried'? This would prove the features are causally active, not just correlated.

children.term: Scale: Does This Hold in Larger Models?
children.definition: GPT-2 Small has 124M parameters. Do the same operator subspaces and case features appear in GPT-2 XL, LLaMA, or Claude? The existence of case-like features in Claude SAEs (Templeton et al. 2024) is suggestive.
